text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Samuel Green, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H734920 NHS No. 7712039845
12 Oak Bank Close, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 4NH

Diagnoses/summary: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal seizure types). History of focal aware auras with evolution to impaired awareness, and separate generalised absence and generalised tonic-clonic events. Prior EEGs have demonstrated generalised spike–wave discharges with occasional left temporal sharp waves. No prior neurosurgery.

Neuroimaging: MRI brain (2021) reported as structurally normal with no focal epileptogenic lesion.

Previous anti-seizure medications: Carbamazepine (worsened absences), Sodium valproate (weight gain, hair thinning), Lamotrigine (partial benefit for focal seizures), Topiramate (cognitive slowing, discontinued), Zonisamide (appetite suppression), Clobazam PRN (sedation), Perampanel (irritability).

Current medication at attendance: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily; Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily; Folic acid 5 mg daily; Thiamine 100 mg daily. No rescue midazolam currently issued.

Allergies: NKDA.

I reviewed Samuel Green in clinic today, who attended alone. He has practiced aura recognition strategies previously and described his warning features in his own words as a “rising wave in the stomach, a brief metallic taste, and a flicker of déjà vu,” typically lasting 10–20 seconds before either settling or progressing to impaired awareness. He also reports separate brief staring spells that family have recognised as absences. He remained seizure-free for five months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure two Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences. He did not sustain injury; post-ictal confusion lasted approximately 30 minutes.

Triggers/safety: He notes poor sleep for several nights preceding the relapse and higher-than-usual work stress. No recent intercurrent illness, alcohol binge, or missed doses by his account. Uses a smartphone diary to log auras and absences. He does not drive and understands DVLA guidance following a generalised seizure. No episodes during swimming or at height; he avoids open water and uses a shower chair as advised previously.

Examination today: Alert and oriented; cranial nerves intact; no focal motor deficit; coordination normal; gait steady. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular. No tongue trauma or shoulder tenderness today.

Investigations: Last routine EEG (2023) showed generalised 3 Hz spike–wave runs with intermittent left temporal sharp waves in drowsiness. Bloods (August 2025) within reference ranges (FBC, U&E, LFT, TSH). Vitamin D borderline low (54 nmol/L), supplementation ongoing.

Counselling provided today: We discussed that his epilepsy is combined generalised and focal in nature, with both generalised and focal seizure types. He articulated his aura features clearly and will continue structured aura recognition and early rest strategies. We reviewed lifestyle factors (sleep, stress management, hydration) and seizure first aid with emphasis on timing, positioning, and avoiding restraint.

Plan:
- Levetiracetam: increase to 750 mg twice daily from tonight, with a view to 1000 mg twice daily if tolerated after two weeks, depending on seizure diary and side effects (irritability, mood change, sleep disturbance discussed).
- Provide buccal midazolam 10 mg as rescue for prolonged convulsive seizure >5 minutes; training leaflet supplied and link to video resource provided.
- Safety and DVLA: Not driving; will follow DVLA regulations. Work note offered if required.
- Monitoring: Nurse specialist telephone review in 2–3 weeks to assess tolerability, mood, and seizure control; earlier contact if mood deterioration occurs. Consider adjunct Lamotrigine re-trial or Valproate only if risk–benefit acceptable and if future control remains suboptimal, noting prior side effects and generalised seizure component.
- Diagnostics: No urgent repeat MRI indicated at present given normal prior scan and typical semiology; consider ambulatory EEG if breakthrough absences persist despite dose optimisation.
- Self-management: Continue aura diary with timestamps, triggers, and post-ictal features. Reinforced sleep hygiene and stress reduction techniques previously taught.

I have requested a routine follow-up in clinic in 3 months, with an option to bring this forward if he has further generalised seizures or significant side effects.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: 1) Identify the explicit time window in the letter: "He remained seizure-free for five months ... before experiencing ..." gives a 5-month interval since starting levetiracetam up to the recent relapse. 2) Identify countable seizure occurrences in that interval as described: one generalised tonic-clonic seizure and one cluster of absences at the time of relapse. 3) The number of absences within the cluster is not specified; to produce a single unified frequency label, treat the occurrence of a cluster as one countable seizure event for normalization purposes. 4) Sum events over the stated 5-month interval: 1 (GTC) + 1 (cluster occurrence) = 2 events in 5 months. 5) The patient is not seizure-free for ≥6 months, so a seizure-free label does not apply. 6) Normalize to the allowed format: 2 per 5 month.
seizure_frequency_number: He remained seizure-free for five months after starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, before experiencing a generalised tonic-clonic seizure two Tuesdays ago, preceded by a cluster of absences.
